description: Pro-innovation approach to AI regulation — a decentralized, sector-specific, principles-based framework. No new primary AI legislation; existing regulators apply 5 cross-sectoral principles within their domains.
regulatoryApproach: Decentralized, sector-specific — existing regulators (Ofcom, FCA, ICO, CMA, MHRA, etc.) interpret and apply principles. Initially non-statutory.

principles.name: Safety, Security, and Robustness
principles.description: AI systems should function in a secure, safe, and robust manner throughout their lifecycle. Risks should be continually identified, assessed, and managed.

principles.name: Appropriate Transparency and Explainability
principles.description: AI systems should be appropriately transparent and explainable. The degree of transparency should be proportionate to the level of risk.

principles.name: Fairness
principles.description: AI systems should not undermine the rights of individuals or organizations, discriminate unfairly, or create unfair outcomes. Regulators may develop domain-specific fairness descriptions.

principles.name: Accountability and Governance
principles.description: Measures should be in place to ensure appropriate oversight of how AI systems are used. Clear lines of accountability should be established.

principles.name: Contestability and Redress
principles.description: Users and affected parties should be able to contest harmful AI decisions and seek redress. Clear routes for challenge should exist.

keyDesignChoices: No new AI-specific regulator (unlike EU AI Office)
Existing sectoral regulators apply principles in their domains
